headline: LCS Cost Now RM11.2 Billion — First Ship in Sea Trials, Programme Cut From 6 to 5
context: The Littoral Combat Ship programme's total cost rose from RM6 billion to RM11.22 billion. Maharaja Lela began sea trials in January 2026; first delivery is now scheduled for December 2026. The programme was reduced from six ships to five.

cards.big: LCS cost has reached RM11.22 billion — and delivery is finally arriving, more than a decade late.
cards.sub: Maharaja Lela began sea trials on 28 January 2026. First delivery is scheduled for December 2026. The programme was cut from six ships to five.

cards.big: Original 2011 budget was RM6 billion; the ceiling escalated to RM9 billion late that year and finally to RM11.22 billion.
cards.sub: PAC's 2022 report documented severe mismanagement and financial issues at Boustead Naval Shipyard before MOF Inc acquired and renamed it Lumut Naval Shipyard in 2024.

cards.big: Multiple senior officers and former Boustead directors have faced MACC investigation and criminal proceedings.
cards.sub: The 2022 PAC findings and subsequent referrals form the basis for ongoing accountability cases.

cards.big: Even with first delivery imminent, the Royal Malaysian Navy has spent more than a decade waiting for vessels Singapore and Indonesia commissioned years ago.
cards.sub: The capability gap during the delay forms the most damaging legacy of the procurement failure.

cards.big: The programme is no longer 'zero ships' — it is 'first ship 15 years late at twice the budget'. The accountability test is whether the 2022 PAC findings produce convictions.
RM11.22 billion and 15 years for the first hull is the real benchmark — not a story of total failure, but of a procurement system that delivers eventually at unacceptable cost.
